name: track-management
description: Use this skill when creating, managing, or working with Conductor
capabilities: Create feature, bug, and refactor tracks with unique identifiers
Generate structured specification documents with requirements and acceptance criteria
Build phased implementation plans with atomic tasks and verification steps
Track progress through status markers and checkpoint commits
Manage track lifecycle from creation through completion
Document and resolve track dependencies and blockers
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All static analysis findings are false positives. The skill consists entirely of markdown documentation files with no executable code. Detected patterns (backticks, SHA references) are markdown formatting and documentation examples, not actual security risks.
valueStatement: Software teams struggle with unclear work boundaries and lost context. Track Management provides a structured system for organizing work into spec-driven, phased tracks with clear progress visibility.
userTitle: Organize and track software work with structured methodology
limitations: Does not execute code or run commands directly
Requires manual creation of files and directories
Does not integrate with external project management tools
Track registry must be maintained manually in markdown

useCases.title: Feature Development Tracking
useCases.description: Break down new features into tracked work units with clear specifications, phased implementation plans, and verifiable acceptance criteria.

useCases.title: Bug Fix Documentation
useCases.description: Document bug reproduction, track fix implementation through phases, and maintain verification records for quality assurance.

useCases.title: Technical Debt Management
useCases.description: Plan and execute refactoring work with clear scope boundaries, risk assessment, and rollback checkpoints.

promptTemplates.title: Create a New Track
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new feature track for [feature name]. Gather requirements, define acceptance criteria, and generate a spec.md with functional and non-functional requirements.
promptTemplates.scenario: Starting work on a new feature with unclear requirements

promptTemplates.title: Generate Implementation Plan
promptTemplates.prompt: Based on the spec.md for [track name], create a phased implementation plan with atomic tasks, verification steps, and estimated effort for each phase.
promptTemplates.scenario: Ready to begin implementation after spec approval

promptTemplates.title: Update Track Progress
promptTemplates.prompt: Update the track status for [track name]. Mark completed tasks with commit SHAs, update phase progress, and identify any blockers or dependencies.
promptTemplates.scenario: Weekly progress update or phase completion

promptTemplates.title: Revert a Track
promptTemplates.prompt: Revert track [track name] to phase [N]. Identify all commits to revert, update status markers, and document the revert reason in the track registry.
promptTemplates.scenario: Feature needs to be rolled back due to issues

bestPractices: Keep tracks focused on a single logical change that can complete in 1-5 days
Include verification tasks after each phase before proceeding
Record commit SHAs for all completed tasks to enable traceability and rollback
antiPatterns: Creating tracks that are too large with multiple unrelated features
Skipping verification tasks to move faster
Not updating status markers immediately after completing work

faq.question: What is the ideal size for a track?
faq.answer: Aim for 1-5 days of work, 2-4 phases, and 8-20 tasks. If your track exceeds this, split it into multiple coherent tracks.

faq.question: How do I handle track dependencies?
faq.answer: Document dependencies in spec.md with type (hard/soft/external), current status, and resolution path. Mark blocked tasks with [!] and the blocking reason.

faq.question: What do the status markers mean?
faq.answer: [ ] pending, [~] in progress, [x] complete (add SHA), [-] skipped, [!] blocked. Update markers immediately as you work.

faq.question: When should I delete versus archive a track?
faq.answer: Archive completed tracks for reference and traceability. Only delete tracks that were created in error or contain sensitive information.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for non-code work?
faq.answer: Yes. The track methodology applies to any structured work requiring specification, planning, and phased execution.

faq.question: How do I revert a track safely?
faq.answer: Use the revert operation to identify commits by track ID, phase, or task. Revert in reverse order, update status markers, and document the revert reason.
